# The Structure of an AI-Assisted Architecture Study {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box, a British statistician who made major contributions to time-series analysis, famously noted that while all models are flawed, the good ones are practically useful. Fast proxies and surrogate models can help architects narrow a search, provided that their scope, calibration, and failure modes are clear. They cannot, on their own, justify a design decision.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter explains what an AI-assisted architecture study must make explicit before an AI method can contribute to a design decision. It connects the study's intent and legal choices to the information AI can inspect, the tools it can use, the evidence its results can support, and the architect responsible for the decision.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must an architect make explicit so that AI can contribute systematically
to a study and its results can support a design decision?
:::

@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales showed why AI-assisted architecture inherits
several problems from current design practice. Relevant information may be
missing or stale. The available choices may be invalid or inadequate. Decisive
evaluation can be expensive, reviewers have limited capacity, and the evidence
may not support a clear next decision. AI can reduce some of this work, but only
when the study defines the part of the problem the method is meant to
help with.

Consider an AI system asked to compare cache configurations for a stated
workload. Before it proposes or evaluates a candidate, it needs the baseline,
the legal cache choices, the performance and power limits, the simulator
settings, and the available evaluation budget. The architect must also decide
what would rule out a candidate and name the person who may act on the results. If any of this
remains implicit, the system can run a coherent study of a different comparison
from the one the architect intended.

Architecture already manages complexity by writing down the relationships that
matter. Architects use models, simulators, workload characterizations, cost
estimates, design rules, and reviews to reason about large design spaces
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. AI adds a new kind of contributor
to that work. It can also produce plausible errors that pass through several
tools before anyone notices them.

Before choosing an AI model, the architect needs to lay out the study. Its
question, alternatives, represented state, allowed actions, tool feedback,
evidence, and decision owner need enough detail for architects and AI systems
to work on the same comparison. The five questions introduced in @sec-moonshot ask
what a system can see and change, how it obtains feedback, which observations
the study trusts as evidence, and what can reject a result. The Architecture 2.0
ontology connects those questions to the challenges from
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales.

Each challenge points to information the study must make explicit
(@tbl-bounds-to-framework).

| **Architecture challenge** | **What the study must make explicit** |
| --- | --- |
| Missing or stale information | Representations and prediction models show what the study can inspect, predict, and constrain. |
| Invalid or inadequate choices | The legal design space and tool environments show which changes are valid and can actually be run. |
| Expensive or inconclusive evaluation | Evaluation budgets, fidelity rules, and evidence records show what the available feedback can support. |
| More results than reviewers can settle | Rejection conditions, checks, and assigned authority show which candidates may continue. |
| No clear next decision | The evidence and named decision owner limit what the study may conclude and what may be authorized next. |

: **From architecture challenges to study structure.** Each row connects a problem established in @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales to the information an AI-assisted study must expose. {#tbl-bounds-to-framework tbl-colwidths="[38,62]"}

The table previews the connections the chapter will make. Intent defines the
task, representations determine what architects and AI systems can see, and
design spaces and tool environments bound what they can do. Evidence rules turn
tool results into support for a claim, rejection checks challenge the result,
and a named owner decides what follows.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can describe the structure of an AI-assisted architecture
study and determine what an AI method needs to do useful work inside it. You can:

- **Bound:** State the study's workload, baseline, design space, constraints, non-goals, and intended decision.
- **Distinguish:** Separate the study, its artifacts, its architectural claim, and the design loop used when work iterates.
- **Connect:** Relate represented state and legal actions to tools, method roles, feedback, and evidence.
- **Assign:** Separate the authority to propose, execute, reject, waive, recommend, and commit.
- **Use:** Apply the ontology and design-loop card without treating either as proof of architectural quality.
:::

## The Architecture 2.0 Ontology {#sec-arch2-ontology}

Architecture has no widely used way to connect the original question,
experiments, evidence, and decisions across tools above RTL. From
RTL down, design flows already use artifacts that tools can parse, transform,
reject, and sign off. High-level synthesis, modeling languages, compiler IRs,
and EDA frameworks also structure parts of the path to implementation. Yet much
of the reasoning about *what* to build remains scattered across whiteboards,
spreadsheets, scripts, slides, meeting notes, and natural-language
specifications.

Compiler infrastructure offers a useful but limited analogy. MLIR
(@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales) exposed many compiler transformations to
tools and reviewers [@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. A universal architecture IR would be
a different goal. The study instead needs a shared description that connects
design intent and workload scope to allowed changes, tool feedback, evidence,
and the next decision.

The resulting structure connects the reasoning that often stays in people's
heads or working notes to implementation flows
(@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl). It lets tools, AI systems, reviewers, and
architects work from the question and comparison that give each artifact or
tool result its meaning.

![**An AI-assisted architecture study connects intent to implementation evidence.** The bounded study contains the scope, architectural claim, execution and analysis process, and evidence needed for review. Iterative work uses a design loop. A compact card indexes these records without replacing them.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="100%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing architecture intent and judgment flowing into a structured architecture study, which connects to executable tools and returns implementation evidence."}

This chapter distinguishes the parts inside that structure. The bounded
architecture study is the unit of work, and it may produce understanding,
artifacts, findings, or decisions. An artifact is an implementation or proposal
from that study. The architectural claim is what another architect evaluates.
When the work iterates, the process used to execute and analyze it is a design
loop. The design-loop card introduced near the end provides an index into the
study's records.

> **Architecture 2.0 ontology.** The Architecture 2.0 ontology is a conceptual
> map of the objects and relationships in a bounded AI-assisted architecture
> study. It connects the study's intent, task, representations, allowed actions,
> tools, method roles, feedback, evidence, claim, and decision authority. It is
> not a formal machine-interpretable ontology.

The ontology follows the engineering work. The stated intent defines the task.
That task determines what information architects and AI systems need and which
changes are allowed. Tools limit those changes and return measurements.
Architects choose methods that fit the work and the available evaluation
budget, then decide which results count as evidence. The evidence limits what
the study can claim, and the assigned decision owner determines what may happen
next.

@fig-ontology-chain follows the study from a scoped question to the next
decision. Representations and environments determine which actions can be run.
Evidence and authority determine which conclusions the study supports and what
may happen next.

![**The ontology connects a scoped study to an architectural decision.** The study establishes scope, the claim defines what must be reviewed, and representations and environments make action possible. When work iterates, a design loop organizes execution and analysis. Evidence supports review by the decision owner, who may advance a finding, narrow the claim, or revise the study.](images/F4-ontology-chain){#fig-ontology-chain width="100%" fig-alt="An ontology connecting a scoped architecture study and its architectural claim to representations, tools, an execution and analysis process, evidence, review by a decision owner, and a scoped finding or decision."}

The two figures answer different questions. @fig-structured-layer-above-rtl
shows where a structured study sits between architecture intent and tool
execution. @fig-ontology-chain maps the chapter from the bounded study through
review and the next decision. It is not a separate method or a step-by-step
procedure. The bounded-study and intent sections develop its first box. The
architectural-claim section develops the second. Representations and tool
interfaces develop the third; the design loop and method roles develop the
fourth; feedback and authority develop the fifth. Design space cuts across the
boxes because it defines which choices are legal. The later reading passes and
design-loop card show how to inspect the whole study.

The boxes in @fig-ontology-chain should look familiar. Architects already do
much of this work manually; the ontology is not claiming that AI invents an
entirely new design process. The difference is how much can remain unstated. An
architect may fill in a missing constraint from experience or remember why an
earlier design was rejected. An AI method cannot reliably use that
project-specific context unless the study makes it available. Scope,
constraints, prior decisions, and evidence therefore need representations the
method can inspect. Tools must expose legal actions and useful results, and the
study must name who may reject a candidate or authorize the next step.
Representations are the connection between the architect's intent and the work
an AI method can perform. Prediction models estimate what may happen after a
proposed change.

Method categories become useful only after these relationships are visible. A
label such as LLM[^fn-ml-llm], reinforcement learning
[^fn-rl-method-label], Bayesian optimization
[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization], or surrogate model does not explain the
architecture work on its own. Nor is a chip-specific language
model[^fn-ml-language-model] a prerequisite for useful AI assistance. Architects
first need to describe the artifacts, allowed changes, tool results, review
criteria, and decision limits in enough detail for a method to act and for
experts to assess its results.

[^fn-ml-llm]: A large language model is a neural network trained on large text corpora to predict and generate language. The label reveals little about an architecture study until the model's role and available state are identified.

[^fn-rl-method-label]: The label "reinforcement learning" does not say what information the AI system received, which actions were legal, how the reward approximated the architecture objective, or which results would rule out a candidate.

[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization]: Bayesian optimization searches for good values of an expensive black-box function while limiting the number of evaluations. In architecture, one evaluation might require a costly cycle-accurate simulation. The method name alone does not reveal the represented state, evaluation budget, or checks used in the study.

[^fn-ml-language-model]: A language model is a statistical model trained to predict the next word or token in a sequence. Modern text-generation systems build on this capability. A domain-specific model is useful only when the architecture study also represents the objects, actions, and evidence the model must use.

## The Bounded Study Is the Unit of Work

> **Bounded architecture study.** A bounded architecture study turns a broad
> goal into a specific piece of architecture work. It states the question,
> workload or scenario, baseline, allowed design choices, objectives,
> constraints, tools, planned evidence, and decision the work is meant to
> inform. It also says what the study will not try to decide.

The lighthouse prompt gives a useful product direction, but it does not yet
define a study. It leaves open which XR workloads matter, what system provides
the baseline, which parts of the design may change, how efficiency will be
measured, and what decision should follow.
One bounded study could compare a defined set of cache organizations for named
XRBench traces against a stated RISC-V baseline, under the 3\ W power
envelope, using an agreed simulator and power model. Its immediate purpose might
be to decide which cache organization, if any, merits more detailed RTL work.
It would not claim to have designed the complete XR subsystem or approved a
product configuration.

The study is the container for that comparison. Simulator runs, generated cache
configurations, plots, and reports are artifacts or evidence inside it. The
design loop is the repeated work used to produce and assess them. A study may
lead to better understanding, an implementation, a map of the design space, a
finding, or a decision, but another architect should always be able to recover
what was compared and what the evidence actually supports.

Bounded does not mean permanently fixed. An architect may discover that another
workload or memory organization matters and expand the work. When the question
changes, the study record should change with it rather than quietly treating
evidence from the original comparison as if it covered the larger question.

## Intent Defines the Task

The lighthouse prompt states the starting intent.

> *Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU,
> accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class low-power
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.*

The product intent is to improve mobile XR efficiency. It is not yet an
architecture task. The architect must decide what work comes next, which might
be characterizing the workload, comparing cache organizations, estimating
memory traffic, or preparing a design-space report. The task is not yet to
generate a complete SoC or approve an RTL change. Choosing the next piece of
work determines what belongs in the study, what can be measured, how costly an
error would be, and how much freedom an AI method should have.

Critiquing a report, predicting energy, searching an accelerator tiling space,
and editing RTL each require different information, tools, and checks. The
architect must therefore define the task before choosing the method.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops the roles AI methods
can perform once that task is clear.

## The Architectural Claim Is the Unit of Review

Asking whether a generative model[^fn-ml-generative-model] can design hardware
is too broad to guide an architecture review. A more useful question asks what
the study found, where that finding applies, and what evidence supports it.
Architects rarely accept an artifact in isolation. They judge what it shows for
a stated workload, baseline, design space, objectives, and constraints. That is
the architectural claim.

[^fn-ml-generative-model]: A generative model produces new data, such as text or configurations, based on patterns learned from its training data. In an architecture design loop, it can propose candidate artifacts inside the legal design space. The study must still test the claim made about each candidate.

> **Architectural claim.** An architectural claim says what the study found and
> where that finding applies. It identifies the workload or scenario, baseline,
> allowed design choices, objectives, constraints, and evidence. It also says
> what the study did not establish and what result would require the finding to
> be narrowed or rejected.

In the lighthouse study, a claim might say that one cache organization reduces
simulated memory stalls for the named XRBench traces relative to the stated
baseline while remaining inside the modeled 3\ W envelope. The claim would be
limited to those traces, simulator settings, and power assumptions. That is its
claim boundary. It would not yet establish post-layout power, timing closure,
or product readiness.

> **Rejection authority, conditions, and checks.** A rejection condition says
> what result would rule out a candidate or narrow a claim. A rejection check is
> the test, tool, rule, or review used to look for that result. Rejection
> authority names the person or policy allowed to stop or redirect the work.
> An automated rule may do so under an agreed policy, but the named decision
> owner remains responsible for exceptions, waivers, and decisions with larger
> consequences.

For the cache study, the stated rules might reject any configuration that
exceeds the 3\ W envelope under the agreed power model. The power-model run is
the check. A script may flag the candidate automatically, but the architect
still decides whether to drop the candidate, investigate the model, or send an
exception for review.

> **Commitment boundary.** The commitment boundary says how far the decision
> owner is prepared to act on the current evidence. It records the next decision
> the evidence supports, not every decision that might eventually follow.

For the same cache result, an early estimate may justify keeping the candidate
in the search but nothing more. Cycle-level simulation and a calibrated power
model may justify selecting it for RTL evaluation, but not choosing a product
configuration. Post-layout or silicon evidence may later support a larger
decision. These are different commitment boundaries for the same design as the
evidence becomes stronger.

The claim boundary and commitment boundary answer different questions. The
claim boundary limits what the result shows. The commitment boundary limits
what the decision owner will authorize on the strength of that result. The
owner remains responsible for any decision that goes beyond what the evidence
alone supports.

The workload or scenario belongs in the claim because evidence from one setting
may not carry into another. Ariane 5 reused inertial-reference software that had
flown on Ariane 4. Those flights showed that the software worked inside the
Ariane 4 flight envelope. They did not show that it would work on Ariane 5's
different, higher-velocity trajectory. During the Ariane 5 launch, the new
trajectory produced a value that overflowed a conversion the older profile had
never reached. Both channels shut down, and the vehicle was lost. The inquiry
traced the failure to the software design and to tests that did not represent
Ariane 5 flight conditions [@Lions1996Ariane5].

The architecture lesson is straightforward. Reusing a trusted component does
not automatically carry its earlier evidence into a new workload or operating
condition. The component must be evaluated again under the conditions in which
it will now be used.

The equal-budget SCALE-Sim study previewed in
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales and executed in @sec-running-the-loop
provides a concrete claim record. Each row of
@tbl-architectural-claim-schema pairs one part of the claim with a review
question and the study's answer.

| **Part of the claim record** | **Reader question** | **SCALE-Sim example** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Claim and limits** | What did the study find, and what did it not establish? | Separate records ask whether a nonbaseline geometry wins, whether the model arm beats the conventional arm, and whether the proposed mechanism predicts the probe. Energy, area, RTL, physical design, and product fitness remain outside the claims. |
| **Comparison boundary** | Which workload, baseline, and design choices define the comparison? | One frozen three-layer GEMM workload, a 32 by 32 baseline, and legal row-by-column geometries under 1,024 processing elements. |
| **Objective and constraints** | What would count as better, and which limits must still hold? | A preregistered cycle-and-utilization score, a 1 percent improvement margin, legal geometry, and a cycle deadline. |
| **Evidence and challenge** | Which results support the finding, and what would weaken it? | Equal SCALE-Sim budgets, every candidate result, replay bindings, and a matched transposition probe. |
| **Decision and authority** | What decision does the evidence support, and who makes it? | The evidence recommends retaining the baseline and stopping; it does not authorize RTL or product action, and the named owner must confirm the decision separately. |

: **Information needed to review an architectural claim.** Each result is reported with its scope, comparison, supporting and challenging evidence, and the decision it can inform. In the executed study, the architecture result, AI contribution, and proposed explanation could be checked separately. The tie and failed explanation therefore remained useful results. {#tbl-architectural-claim-schema tbl-colwidths="[21,31,38]"}

```{=latex}
\Needspace{18\baselineskip}
```

The running cache example makes the possible AI contributions easier to see. A
generative method could propose legal cache configurations. A predictor could
estimate which ones are unlikely to meet the performance or power target before
architects spend time on full simulation. An optimizer could choose which
configuration to evaluate next. The simulator and power model would then test
the candidate, and the agreed checks would determine whether it stays in the
study. The architect would decide whether the results are strong
enough to begin RTL work. Naming the method alone would explain none of this.
The method's role becomes clear only inside the bounded study.

Stating the claim before running the evaluation matters because the same tool
result can mean different things under different workloads, baselines, or
objectives. The claim tells the architect which comparison to run, what can
count as evidence, what would rule out a candidate, and how far the result may
be used. Without that context, a generated design may be technically plausible
without answering the architecture question.

## The Design Loop Is an Execution and Analysis Process

Once the claim is stated, the architect must define how the study will run and
how its results will be analyzed. Iterative work across AI methods and tools
forms a design loop. The loop may frame the problem, choose abstractions,
explore alternatives, measure candidates, reject weak results, revise
assumptions, and decide when the evidence supports the next step.

The loop itself is not new. Architects already move between a design, a tool
result, and the next change. Its purpose here is to show where an AI method
enters that work, what information it can use, what it may change, and where a
reviewer or decision owner still has to judge the result.

> **Architecture design loop.** An architecture design loop is the repeated
> process of examining the current design, making an allowed change, running the
> relevant tools, interpreting the result, and deciding whether to revise,
> reject, or continue. The study records enough of each iteration for another
> architect to see what led to the next step.

To run that loop with AI, the study must say what design information can be read
or changed, which actions are allowed, what the tools return, what counts as
useful evidence, and who can stop or advance the work. If any of this is
missing, reviewers cannot tell how an AI system's output contributed to the
result. The sections that follow unpack the design space, representations, tool
interfaces, method roles, evidence, and authority. The following chapters
develop those parts in greater depth.

Return to the bounded cache study. One iteration begins with the baseline cache
configuration, the allowed changes, and the named XRBench traces. An AI method
proposes a legal configuration, and the simulator measures its performance. The
power model may then show that the candidate exceeds the 3\ W envelope. If so,
the recorded rejection rule removes it from consideration. If it survives, the
architect records the result and decides whether another configuration is worth
trying or whether the evidence is ready for review.

The same pattern applies when the design state is more complex. An architect or
AI method reads the recorded state, makes an allowed change, and runs the
relevant tool. A named reviewer, or an automated check working under a stated
rule, then accepts, rejects, revises, or escalates the candidate. Objectives and
constraints limit the work, while stopping and escalation rules determine
whether another iteration is worthwhile. The state update can be written as
$$
s_{t+1} =
\operatorname{Update}(s_t, a_t, o_t, e_t, d_t).
$$
Here, $s_t$ is the recorded architecture state, $a_t$ is the allowed change,
and $o_t$ is the result returned by the tool environment. The term $e_t$ records
evidence only after the result has been connected to the claim, its meaning has
been explained, and its source has been preserved. Until then, the result
remains feedback and $e_t$ is empty. The term $d_t$ records the decision to
accept, reject, revise, or escalate. This notation does not imply that every
architecture loop is a Markov decision process.[^fn-rl-mdp] Its purpose is to
show which information must be carried into the next iteration. If the study
cannot explain how actions,
feedback, evidence, and decisions update its state, reviewers cannot reconstruct
the work (@fig-design-loop).

[^fn-rl-mdp]: A mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision maker; it forms the foundation of reinforcement learning. The chapter borrows the loop's notation from this framework while cautioning that an architecture loop need not literally be one.

![**How an iterative architecture study proceeds.** An architect or AI method reads the recorded design state, makes an allowed change, runs a tool, and submits the result for review. The reviewer may narrow or accept the claim, reject the candidate, or request another iteration. A higher-fidelity result does not by itself authorize a larger decision.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="100%" fig-alt="A design loop within a scoped architecture study. Represented state, permitted action, tool feedback, and evidence lead to claim review. A scoped finding or decision can leave the loop, while revision updates the study state for another turn."}

The study defines the work, the claim defines what reviewers assess, and the
design loop shows how the work proceeded. An AI model is one contributor inside
that process. It may generate candidates, summarize evidence, predict outcomes,
call tools, critique assumptions, or coordinate subtasks. The study's
comparisons, experiments, and review determine whether those outputs support
the claim.

A microarchitectural choice can affect several layers of the system. It may
require compiler support above the hardware interface or create timing and
routing problems below it. Architects, AI methods, and tools usually see only
part of the cross-layer state. The study must therefore record the assumptions
and consequences that matter at each affected layer.

## Design Spaces Make Claims Meaningful

An architectural claim is meaningful only relative to a design space. A system
that reports "the best" candidate without exposing the alternatives, invalid
regions, baseline, and tradeoffs has hidden the comparison that gives the
result meaning.

In architecture, the design space is not the set of all strings a model might
emit. It is the set of choices allowed by the task and its architecture
constraints.
$$
\mathcal{D} = \{x \in X \mid x \text{ is valid under the task and architecture
constraints}\}.
$$
Here, $X$ includes everything the method could emit before the architecture
constraints are applied. The validity conditions may include ISA compatibility,
memory semantics, software support, timing assumptions, power limits, package constraints,
verification requirements, and deployment policy. The current representation
and toolchain usually support only an executable subset of $\mathcal{D}$. A
candidate inside the legal space but outside that subset may still be a valid
architecture idea; the current tools cannot yet evaluate it. The candidate
should be set aside or evaluated another way rather than labeled invalid. A
candidate outside $\mathcal{D}$ is invalid for the stated study unless the study
revises its scope or constraints and records why.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. A 64-bit RISC-V-based mobile XR
subsystem might vary vector width, cache and memory hierarchy, accelerator
partitioning, dataflow, clocking, voltage assumptions, compiler/runtime
support, and verification scope. Some choices are legal but unattractive. Some
are attractive under a proxy but fail at higher fidelity. Some violate the
power envelope, process assumptions, software contract, or workload coverage.
An Architecture 2.0 study must represent those distinctions. Otherwise its
methods cannot distinguish improvement from exploitation of a hole in the
problem statement.

Most architecture studies must evaluate several objectives at once, including
performance, energy, latency, area, reliability, programmability, verification
effort, and cost. A design-space report should therefore document the
alternatives explored, the tradeoffs observed, the candidates rejected, and the
decisions that remain.

## Representations and Prediction Models

Representation matters because it determines what architects and AI systems
can inspect and use. Architecture knowledge lives in many forms, including
natural-language specifications, ISA documents, traces, graphs, simulator
configurations, RTL, compiler IR, EDA reports, benchmark metadata, scripts, and
review notes. An architect reading a cache spreadsheet may know that a capacity
is per core, remember that the line size is fixed, or recall that a similar
configuration already failed timing. An AI method does not have that context
unless the study records it. Hidden defaults, undocumented constraints, and
forgotten rejections can therefore produce an invalid or misleading result even
when the proposed artifact looks plausible.

In the cache study, the representation would include the baseline
configuration, legal cache parameters, workload traces, simulator and power
model versions, constraints, and results from earlier runs. If the AI method
sees miss rates but not the 3\ W constraint, it may propose a configuration that
looks faster but cannot satisfy the study. The missing information, rather than
the search method, is then the immediate problem.

The representation records the current workload, design state, assumptions,
and earlier results. A prediction model estimates what may happen after
something changes. In the cache example, it might estimate how greater
associativity would affect misses, cycles, and power before a costly full
simulation. The prediction may come from a simulator, learned surrogate,
calibrated equation, or design rule, and its usefulness is limited to the
conditions in which it has been checked.

AI methods often use such models to estimate how represented state may change
after an action [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels;
@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. The model does not need to cover the
entire processor or system. It needs to predict the consequences that matter for
the allowed change and the current task. @sec-data-representations-world-models
develops this idea and introduces the standard terminology.

QuArch, an architecture-centric benchmark dataset, shows why architecture
knowledge alone is not enough. It can assess whether a model knows architecture
concepts, but not whether an AI system has access to the workload, tools,
allowed actions, evidence, and checks needed to synthesize or reject a candidate
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. @sec-data-representations-world-models develops that
distinction.

## Tool Interfaces for AI-Assisted Studies

Representation makes the relevant information available. A tool interface
makes it possible for an AI method to act on that information.

An Architecture 2.0 environment combines an architecture tool with an
interface that defines how an AI system may use it. The interface
specifies legal actions, available observations, execution cost, provenance,
and failure behavior. @fig-tools-become-environments contrasts such an
environment with a basic wrapper that only submits a command and returns a
result.

For the cache study, the interface might allow changes to cache size,
associativity, and replacement policy, then return cycles, misses, estimated
power, run time, and failure messages. It would reject unsupported values and
record the simulator and model versions used for each run. A command wrapper
that returns only a log file would leave much of this behavior implicit.

![**Information required for an executable tool environment.** Legal actions, observations, costs, fidelity, provenance, handling of invalid actions, and failure signals let a reviewer trace tool feedback. The environment does not determine whether that feedback is sufficient evidence or decide the architectural claim.](images/F4e-tools-become-environments){#fig-tools-become-environments width="100%" fig-alt="Side-by-side diagram contrasting a basic tool wrapper with an architecture environment that specifies legal actions, observations, cost and fidelity, provenance, handling of invalid actions, and failure signals."}

ArchGym, an open-source evaluation environment, is an important example because
it treats the connection between search algorithms and architecture simulators
as a first-class interface [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. A simulator wrapper becomes
architecturally useful only when it specifies legal actions,
what happens after an invalid action, feedback cost, provenance, comparable
baselines, and failure signals.
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces expands this point and asks what
such environments can and cannot prove.

## AI Methods Have Different Roles in the Study

The word "agent" says little about what an AI system actually does. An
AI-assisted study may divide the work across several familiar roles. A
generator proposes candidates, a predictor estimates behavior before an
expensive evaluation, and an optimizer chooses what to try next. Critics and
verifiers challenge assumptions and check constraints. Other roles may repair
an artifact that fails a check, explain a tradeoff, or coordinate tool calls.
The important point is to record which role each method plays, what information
it receives, and what it returns. The architect sets the intent, and the named
decision owner decides which evidence is strong enough to act on.

Language models, search procedures, learned surrogates, scripts, formal tools,
and people can perform these roles in different combinations. A reviewer should
be able to tell who or what performed each role, what information it used, what
it changed, and how the result was checked.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops these compositions.

The roles assigned to an AI system do not grant decision authority. Architects
use empirical results to choose objectives and abstractions, weigh cross-layer
tradeoffs, judge acceptable risk, and recommend what to do next. AI may carry
out much of the work and may propose those judgments. A named person or
engineering organization must still take responsibility for the study's
objectives, exceptions, and major decisions. That does not require a person to
approve every configuration or tool call.

## Feedback Becomes Evidence

A represented state, a legal action, and a completed tool run produce feedback.
They do not automatically produce evidence. A simulator result, benchmark
score, synthesis report, generated explanation, or model confidence value is
feedback. It becomes evidence only when the study connects it to a particular
claim, explains the conditions under which it was produced and what it means,
and records where it came from.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="A 3 W claim needs evidence, not a number"}
**Context.** A reported power result is feedback. It becomes evidence only when
the study records enough information for a reviewer to judge what the number
means.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** If a generative method claims that a "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" for the "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
meets the "3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP mobile process," the study must
record the workload, input distribution, memory traffic, power model, process
assumptions, compiled software stack, rejected alternatives, uncertainty, and
the observation or check that would count against the claim.

**Evidence rule.** The same power number supports different decisions depending
on whether it came from a proxy, cycle-level simulation, synthesis, a
post-layout estimate, or silicon. A proxy may be enough to keep a candidate in
the study. Stronger implementation or deployment decisions require evidence
closer to the final system.

The design-space report must say what decision the current evidence supports and
which later decisions still require stronger evidence.
:::

Negative results can also serve as evidence. Rejected candidates, failed
simulator runs, invalid configurations, proxy wins that disappear at higher
fidelity, and abandoned assumptions help map the design space. They show why a
candidate that passed the checks deserves attention and what could overturn the
claim.
@sec-feedback-verification-trust develops the evidence, fidelity, and rejection
rules introduced here.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Feedback and Evidence Check"}
Before advancing a model-generated candidate, ask where the evidence came from,
what result would rule the candidate out, how that result will be checked, who
may stop the work, and who makes the final decision. If the study cannot answer
those questions, the evidence is not yet strong enough for the next step.
:::

## Authority Is Scoped, Not Inherent

Calling a system autonomous or non-autonomous tells us too little. Authority is
assigned for a particular action; it does not come from the model's capability.
A study therefore records separate decision rights instead of giving the system
one overall level of automation (@tbl-decision-rights-matrix).

| **Right** | **What it authorizes** | **Required record** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Propose** | Produce a candidate, hypothesis, test, or change for review. | Person or system, allowed output type, scope, budget, and required provenance. |
| **Execute** | Run a declared action inside the environment. | Legal actions, resource limits, stop conditions, failure handling, and rollback path. |
| **Reject** | Block a candidate, claim, or next step when a named rejection check fails. | Check, criterion, evidence source, independence relationship, and rejector. |
| **Waive** | Permit a failed or unmet rejection check to be bypassed under stated conditions. | Waiver holder, rationale, expiration, residual risk, and reopening condition. |
| **Recommend** | Summarize the evidence and recommend a specific next step. | Recommender, supported scope, alternatives, what the recommendation does not cover, and uncertainty. |
| **Commit** | Approve the next high-impact step and accept its remaining risk. | Decision owner, approved step, accepted evidence, and conditions for reversal or revalidation. |

: **Decision rights are independent assignments.** A person or system may hold
one right without holding the others. For each right, the study should state its
scope, required evidence, and consequences. A model's technical capability does
not by itself grant authority. {#tbl-decision-rights-matrix tbl-colwidths="[14,34,42]"}

The separation appears in ordinary workflows. A model may propose a cache
configuration, a harness may execute it, and an automated check may reject it
when it fails the agreed threshold. A review lead may decide whether to allow an
exception, an architect may recommend a tradeoff, and a product organization
may decide whether to commit resources to RTL implementation. One person or
system can hold several rights, but holding one does not automatically grant
another.

Architects remain responsible for framing the study and authorizing major next
steps (@sec-what-architect-owns). This does not limit the work an AI method may
perform. It identifies who must answer for the comparison, the evidence, and the
consequences. A card can show that the assignments were recorded, but it cannot
prove that the reviewer is sufficiently independent or that the evidence is
strong enough.

The appropriate scope depends on architecture-specific risk. A compiler flag
can often be rolled back after deployment telemetry, while an RTL change may
affect timing closure and a mask-level choice may be irreversible. As the cost
of reversal rises, the study needs stronger evidence, more independent
challenge, and a named decision owner.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint}
Before assigning execute, reject, waive, recommend, or commit rights, ask what
actions are allowed, what evidence is required, when the work must be escalated,
who owns the decision, and what happens if the decision is wrong. If the study
cannot answer those questions, it is not ready to grant the right.
:::

## Reading the Study in Three Passes

Once the question, claim, design loop, design space, tools, roles, and evidence
are clear, a reader can work through the study in three passes. The first pass
checks what the study set out to compare. The second checks what the architects
and AI methods could see, change, and evaluate. The third asks what the evidence
supports and what the decision owner may authorize next. These are reading
passes, not fixed stages in the work.

An early demonstration may not have every answer. It can still be useful, but
its claim must remain narrow enough to match the information and evidence that
are available. The reader should be able to see what remains unknown and which
decisions the demonstration cannot yet support.

@tbl-compact-framework-checklist applies the three passes to the running cache
example.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

| **Reading pass** | **Reader question** | **Lighthouse instance** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Scope** | What does the study set out to show, for which workload and baseline, and among which design choices? | Compare a defined set of cache organizations for named XRBench traces against a stated RISC-V baseline under the 3\ W envelope, then decide whether one merits RTL evaluation. |
| **Execution** | What information can the AI methods use, what may they change, which roles do they play, and which tools and evaluation budget are available? | Workload traces, legal cache parameters, compiler assumptions, a simulator and power model, candidate generation or search, and a declared simulation budget. |
| **Judgment** | Which results support or challenge the finding, why did the result occur, and what may the decision owner authorize next? | Performance and power results, rejected configurations, sensitivity analysis, an explanation based on misses and memory traffic, and a decision about further RTL work. |

: **Three passes through the framework.** Scope, execution, and judgment each pair the reader's question with the corresponding lighthouse instance. {#tbl-compact-framework-checklist tbl-colwidths="[20,36,34]"}

This order prevents the name or capability of an AI method from becoming a
substitute for a defined comparison and adequate evidence.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

Standard AI terms become useful when the reader can connect them to specific
architecture artifacts, actions, measurements, and checks
(@tbl-architecture-translation-matrix). An architecture study states its
performance, power, and area objectives rather than referring only to a generic
reward. If a paper says an AI method works in an environment, the reader should
be able to name the architecture information it receives, the changes it may
make, the feedback its tools return, what would count against the result, and
who decides what happens next.

| **Generic term** | **Architecture translation** | **Example artifacts or observations** | **Failure or challenge condition** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **State** | Workload, design, software, tool, constraint, and evidence state. | Traces, configs, RTL, compiler IR, simulator stats, EDA reports, review notes. | Missing provenance or hidden assumptions. |
| **Action** | Legal architecture, compiler, runtime, or tool-flow change. | Change cache size, vector width, mapping, schedule, constraint, partition, or test. | Invalid parameter, noncompilable code, nonsynthesizable RTL, or policy violation. |
| **Observation** | Feedback returned by a tool, benchmark, review, or deployment path. | Latency, energy, area, timing, congestion, warnings, failures, telemetry. | Wrong workload, stale tool version, simulator mismatch, or weak fidelity. |
| **Environment** | Tool-connected harness that defines legal actions and feedback. | Simulator wrapper, compiler pipeline, RTL flow, EDA stage, benchmark harness. | Unmodeled constraints, nondeterminism, incomplete logging, or invalid actions. |
| **Objective** | Stated architecture tradeoff, not a generic reward. | Performance, power, and area; tail latency; power envelope; reliability; carbon; cost; evidence budget. | Proxy gaming, lost Pareto tradeoff, or missing decision rule. |
| **Critic/verifier** | Declared challenge check that can weaken or reject a claim; independence is required when the consequence warrants it. | Tests, formal checks, baseline replay, cross-simulator comparison, signoff review. | Unsupported claim, failed check, counterexample, or insufficient evidence. |

: **AI terms need architecture translations.** Architecture 2.0 uses generic vocabulary only when each term is grounded in concrete hardware/software design objects, tool outputs, and stated ways to check the result. {#tbl-architecture-translation-matrix tbl-colwidths="[16,28,28,18]"}

The architect may revisit any of these relationships as the study proceeds. A
failed simulation may expose missing design information. A weak benchmark
result may require a different task. A problem with where a result came from may
make the evidence unusable, while an invalid candidate may expose a missing tool
check. The study can change, but the changes and their effects should remain
visible, and named people remain responsible for decisions.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Study Revision Check"}
When an AI method fails or produces an invalid result, the architect must decide
whether to reject only that candidate or to revise the tools, design space, or
evidence needed by the study.
:::

## What Other Fields Make Explicit

Architecture does not need to import another field's institutions or vocabulary
unchanged, but several fields have learned to make complex claims and workflows
easier to inspect. Popper's work offers one starting point. A claim should say
what evidence would support it and what result would show that it needs to be
revised or rejected.[^fn-popper-falsifiability]

[^fn-popper-falsifiability]: Popper developed the criterion in *Logik der
Forschung*, published in German in 1934 and later in English as *The Logic of
Scientific Discovery* [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. Here
falsifiability belongs to an architectural claim and its test protocol, not to
an AI model as an intrinsic property. It asks what observable result would
conflict with the claim. It does not imply that one failed run establishes the
root cause or ends the inquiry.

Most architecture decisions are not universal scientific hypotheses. The
useful lesson is narrower. State the scope, name the observations that would
count against the claim, and allow stronger evidence to reopen the decision.
@tbl-cross-field-method-lineage compares what several established fields record,
what transfers to Architecture 2.0, and where each analogy stops.

| **External practice** | **What it records** | **What transfers to Architecture 2.0** | **Where the analogy stops** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Experimental science | A claim, test conditions, observations that could conflict with it, and provisional rather than final acceptance [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. | State what the study does and does not claim, say in advance what would count against it, and preserve results that weaken the preferred explanation. | Tool failure, model mismatch, and scope error can also explain a failed run, so rejection still requires diagnosis. |
| Experimental science: high-energy physics | Data, code, computational environment, workflow steps, provenance, versions, and internal review state captured during the analysis [@ChenEtAl2016CERNAnalysisPreservation]. | Save versions of the designs and runnable inputs while the work is happening rather than reconstructing them after publication. | A preserved workflow can support replay without establishing that its claim, baseline, or decision was adequate. |
| Experimental science: translational bioinformatics failure analysis | Raw data, transformations, labels, and code needed to reconstruct a predictor after undocumented processing hid simple errors; in the cited case, trial allocation was already occurring [@BaggerlyCoombes2009]. | Record each transformation and make sure the result can be challenged before it is used for a decision. | The cited case exposed failure after clinical use began; it does not show that pre-use independent reanalysis was already standard practice. |
| Systems safety and assurance | System constraints, process assumptions, control and feedback paths, evidence arguments, responsibility, and waivers [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld; @Kelly2004GSN]. | Review the whole process, including people, tools, and policies, rather than treating a capable model or passing component check as sufficient. | Most architecture exploration is not safety certification; evidence and independence should scale with consequence. |
| Site-reliability engineering | Observability, error budgets, runbooks, rollback, and revalidation under drift [@BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. | Expand automated authority where actions are monitored and reversible, and narrow it as rollback becomes costly. | Silicon, interfaces, and long-lived hardware commitments are not as cheaply reversible as software deployments. |
| Scientific databases and benchmarks | Shared data, metadata, interfaces, workloads, and reporting rules that let results travel across groups [@JainEtAl2013MaterialsProject; @SPEC2017CPU; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. | Build common datasets and comparison protocols around stated task and evidence scope. | A dataset or leaderboard alone does not record why a design was rejected or who authorized the resulting claim. |

: **Relevant practices from other fields.** Architecture 2.0 draws on these practices without importing another field's institutions or vocabulary unchanged. {#tbl-cross-field-method-lineage tbl-colwidths="[18,27,29,18]"}

EDA does not appear in the table because it is already part of computer
architecture practice rather than an external influence. The examples also
show why logs alone are insufficient. Logs can preserve observations and
provenance, but a reviewer still needs the question, alternatives,
results that challenge the claim, proposed explanation, review process, and
decision authority.

## The Design-Loop Card

> **Design-loop card.** A design-loop card is a short guide to one architecture
> study. It points to the question, scope, claim, way the work was run,
> supporting and challenging evidence, stopping rules, next decision supported
> by the evidence, and person responsible for that decision. Detailed design
> state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.

The card gives reviewers a short way to inspect a paper, project, benchmark, or
internal study while the full evidence remains in linked artifacts. An author
may fill it in while conducting a study, or a reader may use it to find missing
information.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The missing record became part of the disagreement"}
**The claim.** The AlphaChip paper reported a learned macro-placement method
that produced results comparable to or better than expert placement
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** Later researchers could not reconstruct every proprietary design,
training detail, and evaluation condition behind the strongest reported
results. The original authors argued that the reassessment had not run the
method as described
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL;
@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** A design-loop card would not settle that disagreement, but it
would show which baseline, scope, tool path, and evidence records were shared
and which were missing.
:::

For the lighthouse prompt, one card points to the claim, the study boundary,
the tools and AI roles used, and the records that support the next decision
(@fig-design-loop-card-example). The underlying artifacts remain in their
source files.

![**A design-loop card indexes one architecture study.** It points readers to the study scope, claim, execution process, evidence, and decision owner. Detailed state, evidence, and replay artifacts remain in linked records.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="100%" fig-alt="A compact study index card for the lighthouse prompt, listing the twelve canonical card fields from intent and task through failed runs, rejection checks and authority, the evidence-supported claim boundary, and the accountable decision, under a claim banner, with a footer noting that the card indexes rather than replaces the study records."}

The card reveals whether a paper or project gives reviewers enough information
about its study and claim. A paper is hard to compare when it reports a better
search result but omits the cost of feedback, rejected candidates, or limits of
the environment. A tool is hard to assess when it produces designs without
stating which checks can rule them out. A benchmark may measure model accuracy without
testing architecture-relevant reasoning; that can still be useful, but it is
not a complete architecture study.

A missing field calls for more evidence or a narrower claim rather than
automatic rejection. A complete card can still describe weak architecture, so
completion does not establish quality, novelty, insight, or correctness. The
first open question below asks whether card-based review catches missing
evidence more consistently than ordinary review.

@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full twelve-field card and review
rubric. Detailed evidence and replay packages remain linked artifacts rather
than additional card fields. A replay package records executable inputs, tool
and environment versions, parameters, and outputs for an attempted rerun. A
major Architecture 2.0 claim should provide enough of its study for another
architect to reconstruct and challenge it.

The appendix also gives the card a machine-checkable schema. Separate profiles
cover context, inspectability, replay, independent review, disclosure, and
decision rights. A validator can check that the required fields and links are
present, but passing one profile does not imply that the others will pass. A
complete schema also says nothing by itself about the quality of the evidence.

@sec-data-representations-world-models,
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces,
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, and
@sec-feedback-verification-trust examine the information indexed by the card in
greater detail.

## Open Research Questions

The research questions below ask how much information reviewers need, which
workload and baseline records make a claim credible, and whether structured
review can identify problems before expensive execution.

1. **What minimum record lets another architect review AI-assisted work?**
An AI-assisted architecture study should state its question, workload scope,
baseline, design space, represented state, environment, method assignments,
evidence for and against the claim, challenge process, decision rights, and
commitment boundary. A useful study would test how much of that information can
be indexed without exposing the author's full internal toolchain. Independent
reviewers could then assess the same work with and without the index and compare
how often they find missing evidence, decisions that reach beyond the evidence,
and steps whose reasoning cannot be reviewed. The design-loop card in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card provides one starting point.

2. **What workload and baseline records make a claim credible?**
Another architect can review workload characterization when a paper records
input distributions, benchmark construction, excluded cases, holdout workloads,
software versions, baseline assumptions, and provenance. Research on this
question could define a standard set of workload and baseline records for
AI-assisted architecture claims, then test whether it helps reviewers
distinguish results that generalize from those that do not. This treatment makes
workload scope part of the claim rather than background context.

3. **How can holdout benchmarks detect study or method overfitting?**
An AI-assisted study can overfit its methods and conclusions to a benchmark
suite, trace slice, proxy model, or metric used during development. Research on
holdout construction should test whether unseen workloads, changed baselines,
or higher-fidelity feedback expose conclusions that do not transfer. The goal
is to distinguish a learned architectural relationship from a method that has
adapted to the evaluation setup.

4. **How can rejected alternatives help document the design space?**
Rejected candidates help bound the claim because they show which regions of
the design space were considered and why they failed. A research contribution
could define a common record for rejected alternatives, invalid actions, failed
timing closures, proxy failures, and repaired candidates. Researchers could then
measure whether reviewers can reconstruct the boundary of the explored design
space from that record. This question extends the ontology in
@sec-arch2-ontology to negative evidence.

5. **What can structured review catch before expensive execution?**
A study record should be useful before tools run, not only after a result
exists. An experiment could test whether missing baselines, invalid action
spaces, weak evidence rules, or overly broad commitment boundaries predict
failure once the tools run. A static checker, review process, authoring tool, or
design-loop card could flag those problems while leaving architectural judgment
with the reviewers.

## Conclusion

Systematic AI assistance begins with a bounded architecture study. The architect
turns a broad goal into a specific comparison, states what the study is meant to
show, and records the workload, baseline, allowed design choices, tools,
evidence, and next decision. AI methods can then take on defined roles
without being mistaken for the study itself.

Those activities are familiar. What changes when AI participates is that the
information it can use, the actions it may take, the feedback it receives, and
the decisions its results may support must be visible rather than assumed.

The same structure makes the result easier to trust and use. Another architect
can see what the AI system knew, what it was allowed to change, how the tools
evaluated its work, what evidence supports the finding, and who is responsible
for deciding what happens next.

The chapter keeps three concepts distinct.

- The **bounded study** is the unit of work.
- The **architectural claim** is the finding another architect evaluates,
  together with its workload, baseline, design space, limits, and evidence.
- The **design loop** is the repeated execution and analysis used to carry out
  the work.

A study may end in better understanding, an artifact, a finding, or a decision.
The ontology, design-loop card, evidence records, checks, and replay package
help another architect review that outcome at different depths. None of them is
a substitute for architectural judgment.

Review and replay answer different questions. A successful replay shows that
the recorded inputs, tool settings, and outputs can be reproduced. It does not
show that the comparison was the right one or that the evidence supports the
decision.

The ontology and card do not guarantee correctness or architectural value. They
help another architect determine what information the study used, what it
measured, how the result was challenged, and who made the decision. The same
questions apply across different models and tools.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Keep the three concepts distinct. The bounded study defines the work, the architectural claim states what another architect must evaluate, and the design loop is the execution and analysis process for iterative work.
- **Reader test:** Can you identify the architecture question, AI role,
  alternatives, tool-connected experiments, claim, results that challenge it,
  explanation, and next decision?
- **Up next:** @sec-data-representations-world-models asks what representations and prediction models must encode before AI methods can act on architecture state credibly.
:::
